expanded_description: A comprehensive guide to drawing Lewis structures for covalent compounds, showing how atoms share electrons to form molecules with complete octets, including single, double, and triple bonds, formal charge calculations, and resonance structures.

core_concept.principle: Lewis structures for covalent compounds show how atoms share valence electrons through covalent bonds to achieve stable octets, with lines representing shared electron pairs and dots representing lone pairs.
core_concept.explanation: Covalent Lewis structures differ from ionic structures because they show electron sharing rather than transfer. Atoms are connected by lines (each line = 2 shared electrons), and unshared valence electrons appear as dots (lone pairs). The goal is to arrange electrons so each atom (except H) has 8 electrons around it. Multiple bonds (double, triple) form when single bonds don't provide enough electrons for octets. Formal charge calculations help identify the most stable structure when multiple arrangements are possible.

image.alt: Lewis structures showing H₂O, CO₂, and NH₃ with shared bonds and lone pairs
image.caption: Covalent Lewis structures: lines = shared bonds, dots = lone pairs, all atoms achieve octets.

sections.section_title: Steps for Drawing Lewis Structures for Covalent Compounds
sections.introduction: Drawing Lewis structures for covalent compounds follows a systematic procedure that ensures all valence electrons are accounted for and all atoms achieve stable electron configurations.

key_concept.definition: A systematic 5-step procedure for drawing Lewis structures: (1) Count total valence electrons, (2) Arrange atoms with least electronegative as center, (3) Draw single bonds, (4) Distribute remaining electrons as lone pairs, (5) Form multiple bonds if octets incomplete.
key_concept.context: This procedure works for most covalent molecules; exceptions include expanded octets, incomplete octets, and odd-electron molecules.

principles.statement: Add up valence electrons from all atoms; add electrons for negative charges, subtract for positive charges.
principles.application: Ensures all electrons are accounted for; essential for correct structure.
principles.example: H₂O: 2(H) × 1 + 1(O) × 6 = 8 valence e⁻. CO₂: 1(C) × 4 + 2(O) × 6 = 16 valence e⁻. NH₄⁺: 1(N) × 5 + 4(H) × 1 - 1(charge) = 8 valence e⁻.
principles.analogy: Like counting puzzle pieces before starting: you need to know how many pieces (electrons) you have to work with.

principles.statement: Place least electronegative atom (except H) in center; H and F are always terminal (outer) atoms.
principles.application: Determines molecular skeleton; central atom typically forms most bonds.
principles.example: H₂O: O center (H can't be center). CO₂: C center (less electronegative than O). CH₄: C center with 4 H around.
principles.analogy: Like a hub and spokes: central atom is the hub, other atoms are spokes radiating outward.

principles.statement: Connect atoms with single bonds (lines); each bond uses 2 electrons.
principles.application: Creates molecular framework; subtract bond electrons from total.
principles.example: H₂O: 2 O-H bonds use 4 e⁻ (8 - 4 = 4 remaining). CO₂: 2 C-O bonds use 4 e⁻ (16 - 4 = 12 remaining).
principles.analogy: Like building a frame: bonds are the structural supports holding atoms together.

principles.statement: Place remaining electrons as lone pairs on outer atoms first to complete their octets, then on central atom.
principles.application: Satisfies octet rule for outer atoms; any remaining go to central atom.
principles.example: H₂O: 4 e⁻ remaining → 2 lone pairs on O. CO₂: 12 e⁻ remaining → 6 lone pairs on O atoms (3 per O).
principles.analogy: Like seating guests: fill outer seats (outer atoms) first, then central seats (central atom).

principles.principle: Step 5: Form Multiple Bonds if Needed
principles.statement: If central atom lacks octet, move lone pairs from outer atoms to form double or triple bonds.
principles.application: Completes octets for all atoms; creates multiple bonds when necessary.
principles.example: CO₂: C has only 4 e⁻ after step 4; move 1 lone pair from each O to form C=O double bonds. C now has 8 e⁻.
principles.analogy: Like sharing resources: outer atoms 'lend' electron pairs to central atom to complete its octet.

components.definition: Lewis structure drawing procedure with electron counting and octet verification
components.interpretation: Count all valence electrons first; use 2 per bond; remaining become lone pairs; form multiple bonds if central atom lacks octet.

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for Water (H₂O)
worked_examples.problem: Draw the complete Lewis structure for water (H₂O) following all 5 steps. Show all valence electrons, bonds, and lone pairs.

step_by_step.explanation: Count total valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: H: 2 atoms × 1 e⁻ = 2 e⁻; O: 1 atom × 6 e⁻ = 6 e⁻; Total = 8 valence electrons

step_by_step.explanation: Arrange atoms with O as center (H can only form 1 bond).
step_by_step.calculation: H-O-H arrangement (O center, 2 H terminal)

step_by_step.explanation: Draw single bonds between O and each H.
step_by_step.calculation: 2 bonds × 2 e⁻ = 4 e⁻ used; 8 - 4 = 4 e⁻ remaining

step_by_step.explanation: Place remaining electrons as lone pairs on O (H already has duet).
step_by_step.calculation: 4 e⁻ remaining = 2 lone pairs on O; O now has 8 e⁻ (2 bonds + 2 lone pairs)

step_by_step.explanation: Check if multiple bonds needed.
step_by_step.calculation: O has 8 e⁻ ✓, each H has 2 e⁻ ✓; No multiple bonds needed; Structure complete

worked_examples.final_answer: H₂O Lewis structure: H-O-H with 2 lone pairs on O. Total 8 valence electrons used (4 in bonds, 4 in lone pairs). O has octet (8 e⁻), each H has duet (2 e⁻). Bent molecular geometry results from lone pair repulsion.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic 5-step Lewis structure procedure for simple covalent molecule

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for CO₂. Show why double bonds are necessary to satisfy the octet rule for all atoms.

step_by_step.explanation: Count total valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: C: 4 e⁻; O: 2 atoms × 6 e⁻ = 12 e⁻; Total = 16 valence electrons

step_by_step.explanation: Arrange atoms with C as center (less electronegative than O).
step_by_step.calculation: O-C-O arrangement (C center, 2 O terminal)

step_by_step.explanation: Draw single bonds first.
step_by_step.calculation: 2 bonds × 2 e⁻ = 4 e⁻ used; 16 - 4 = 12 e⁻ remaining

step_by_step.explanation: Place remaining electrons as lone pairs on O atoms.
step_by_step.calculation: 12 e⁻ = 6 lone pairs; 3 pairs per O; C has only 4 e⁻ (2 bonds) → incomplete octet!

step_by_step.explanation: Form double bonds to complete C octet.
step_by_step.calculation: Move 1 lone pair from each O to form C=O double bonds; C now has 8 e⁻ (4 bonds); each O has 8 e⁻ (2 bonds + 2 lone pairs)

worked_examples.final_answer: CO₂ Lewis structure: O=C=O with 2 lone pairs on each O. Double bonds are necessary because single bonds leave C with only 4 electrons. Linear geometry (180° bond angle). All atoms satisfy octet rule. Total 16 valence electrons used (8 in bonds, 8 in lone pairs).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Multiple bond formation to satisfy octet rule in Lewis structures

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for Ammonia (NH₃)
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for ammonia (NH₃). Show all bonds, lone pairs, and verify octet/duet rule satisfaction.

step_by_step.explanation: Count total valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: N: 5 e⁻; H: 3 atoms × 1 e⁻ = 3 e⁻; Total = 8 valence electrons

step_by_step.explanation: Arrange atoms with N as center (H can only form 1 bond).
step_by_step.calculation: N center with 3 H atoms around it

step_by_step.explanation: Draw single bonds between N and each H.
step_by_step.calculation: 3 bonds × 2 e⁻ = 6 e⁻ used; 8 - 6 = 2 e⁻ remaining

step_by_step.explanation: Place remaining electrons as lone pair on N.
step_by_step.calculation: 2 e⁻ = 1 lone pair on N; N now has 8 e⁻ (3 bonds + 1 lone pair); each H has 2 e⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Check if multiple bonds needed.
step_by_step.calculation: N has 8 e⁻ ✓, each H has 2 e⁻ ✓; No multiple bonds needed; Structure complete

worked_examples.final_answer: NH₃ Lewis structure: N center with 3 H atoms single-bonded, 1 lone pair on N. Total 8 valence electrons used (6 in bonds, 2 in lone pair). N has octet (8 e⁻), each H has duet (2 e⁻). Trigonal pyramidal geometry results from lone pair repulsion.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Lewis structure with lone pair on central atom affecting molecular geometry

sections.section_title: Single, Double, and Triple Bonds in Lewis Structures
sections.introduction: Covalent bonds can involve sharing one, two, or three electron pairs. The number of shared pairs affects bond strength, bond length, and molecular properties. Lewis structures represent these as single, double, or triple lines.

key_concept.definition: Bond multiplicity in Lewis structures: single bond (1 line = 2 e⁻ shared), double bond (2 lines = 4 e⁻ shared), triple bond (3 lines = 6 e⁻ shared).
key_concept.context: Multiple bonds form when single bonds don't provide enough electrons for all atoms to achieve octets; triple bonds are strongest and shortest, single bonds are weakest and longest.

classifications.value: One shared electron pair (2 electrons) represented by one line
classifications.behavior: Formed when atoms share one electron pair; allows rotation; sufficient for many molecules.

examples.explanation: Two H atoms share 1 electron pair: H-H. Each H achieves duet (2 e⁻). Bond length = 74 pm, bond energy = 436 kJ/mol.

image.alt: H₂ molecule showing single covalent bond
image.caption: Single bond: one shared pair, each H achieves duet.

examples.explanation: CH₄ has four C-H single bonds. C shares 1 e⁻ pair with each of 4 H atoms. C achieves octet (8 e⁻); each H achieves duet (2 e⁻).

image.alt: Methane Lewis structure with four C-H single bonds
image.caption: Methane: C forms 4 single bonds to achieve octet.

examples.explanation: Ethane (C₂H₆) has C-C single bond connecting two carbons. Free rotation around C-C bond. Bond length = 154 pm, bond energy = 348 kJ/mol.

image.alt: Ethane Lewis structure showing C-C single bond
image.caption: C-C single bond: allows rotation, creates different conformations.

classifications.value: Two shared electron pairs (4 electrons) represented by two lines
classifications.behavior: Formed when atoms need to share 4 electrons to complete octets; creates rigid structures; common in C=O, C=C, O=O.

examples.explanation: Two O atoms share 2 electron pairs: O=O. Each O has 6 valence e⁻, needs 2 more. Bond length = 121 pm, bond energy = 498 kJ/mol.

image.alt: O₂ molecule showing double covalent bond
image.caption: Double bond: two shared pairs, stronger and shorter than single.

examples.explanation: CO₂ has two C=O double bonds: O=C=O. C shares 2 e⁻ pairs with each O. Linear molecule, bond length = 116 pm, bond energy = 799 kJ/mol per C=O.

image.alt: CO₂ Lewis structure with two C=O double bonds
image.caption: CO₂: two double bonds give C an octet, linear geometry.

examples.explanation: Ethene (C₂H₄) has C=C double bond. No rotation creates cis/trans isomerism. Bond length = 134 pm (shorter than C-C 154 pm).

image.alt: Ethene Lewis structure showing C=C double bond
image.caption: C=C double bond: rigid, prevents rotation, enables geometric isomers.

classifications.value: Three shared electron pairs (6 electrons) represented by three lines
classifications.behavior: Formed when atoms need to share 6 electrons to complete octets; very strong; rare but important.

examples.explanation: Two N atoms share 3 electron pairs: N≡N. Each N has 5 valence e⁻, needs 3 more. Bond length = 110 pm, bond energy = 945 kJ/mol (strongest covalent bond).

image.alt: N₂ molecule showing triple covalent bond
image.caption: Triple bond: three shared pairs, strongest covalent bond, explains N₂ inertness.

examples.explanation: Ethyne (acetylene, C₂H₂) has C≡C triple bond. Linear geometry (180°). Bond length = 120 pm, bond energy = 839 kJ/mol. Used in welding torches.

image.alt: Ethyne Lewis structure showing C≡C triple bond
image.caption: C≡C triple bond: linear geometry, very strong, used in acetylene torches.

examples.explanation: Hydrogen cyanide (HCN) has C≡N triple bond. Linear molecule. C shares 3 pairs with N, 1 pair with H. Important in organic chemistry.

image.alt: HCN Lewis structure showing C≡N triple bond
image.caption: HCN: C≡N triple bond, linear geometry, important in organic synthesis.

principles.principle: Bond Length and Bond Energy Relationship
principles.statement: Bond length decreases and bond energy increases with bond multiplicity: triple < double < single (length); triple > double > single (energy).
principles.application: Predicts molecular stability and reactivity; shorter, stronger bonds are harder to break.
principles.example: C-C: 154 pm, 348 kJ/mol; C=C: 134 pm, 614 kJ/mol; C≡C: 120 pm, 839 kJ/mol. Triple bond is 2.4× stronger than single.
principles.analogy: Like ropes: one rope (single) is weaker; three ropes bundled (triple) are stronger and shorter.

principles.statement: Single bonds allow free rotation; double and triple bonds are rigid and prevent rotation, creating fixed geometries.
principles.application: Explains molecular conformations (single) vs. geometric isomers (double); affects molecular shape and properties.
principles.example: Ethane (C-C): free rotation creates different conformations. Ethene (C=C): rigid, creates cis/trans isomers with different properties.
principles.analogy: Like a door hinge: single = door swings freely; double = door welded shut (fixed).

image.alt: Diagram comparing rotation around single vs. double bonds
image.caption: Bond rotation: single bonds rotate freely; double/triple bonds are rigid.

worked_examples.title: Comparing Bond Types in Different Molecules
worked_examples.problem: Compare the bond types, lengths, and energies in: (a) C₂H₆ (ethane), (b) C₂H₄ (ethene), (c) C₂H₂ (ethyne). Explain the relationship between bond multiplicity and properties.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify the C-C bond type in each molecule.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) C₂H₆: C-C single bond; (b) C₂H₄: C=C double bond; (c) C₂H₂: C≡C triple bond

step_by_step.explanation: Compare bond lengths.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) C-C: 154 pm; (b) C=C: 134 pm; (c) C≡C: 120 pm. Triple < Double < Single

step_by_step.explanation: Compare bond energies.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) C-C: 348 kJ/mol; (b) C=C: 614 kJ/mol; (c) C≡C: 839 kJ/mol. Triple > Double > Single

step_by_step.explanation: Compare bond rotation capability.
step_by_step.calculation: (a) C-C: free rotation; (b) C=C: no rotation (rigid); (c) C≡C: no rotation (rigid)

worked_examples.final_answer: Bond multiplicity relationship: (a) Ethane C-C single: longest (154 pm), weakest (348 kJ/mol), free rotation. (b) Ethene C=C double: intermediate (134 pm, 614 kJ/mol), rigid. (c) Ethyne C≡C triple: shortest (120 pm), strongest (839 kJ/mol), rigid. More shared electron pairs = stronger attraction = shorter, stronger, more rigid bond.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Bond multiplicity effects on bond length, energy, and molecular flexibility

sections.section_title: Formal Charge and Lewis Structure Selection
sections.introduction: When multiple valid Lewis structures can be drawn for a molecule, formal charge calculations help identify the most stable structure. The best Lewis structure minimizes formal charges and places negative charges on more electronegative atoms.

key_concept.definition: Formal charge is the hypothetical charge on an atom in a molecule, calculated as: Formal Charge = Valence e⁻ - (Lone pair e⁻ + ½ Bonding e⁻). The best Lewis structure has formal charges closest to zero.
key_concept.context: Formal charge helps distinguish between resonance structures and identifies the most stable arrangement when multiple Lewis structures are possible.

principles.statement: Formal charge = valence electrons - (lone pair electrons + ½ bonding electrons); sum of all formal charges equals overall molecular charge.
principles.application: Identifies most stable Lewis structure; helps predict charge distribution in molecules and ions.
principles.example: CO₂: C formal charge = 4 - (0 + ½×8) = 0; O formal charge = 6 - (4 + ½×4) = 0. All formal charges = 0, confirming O=C=O is correct.
principles.analogy: Like balancing a budget: formal charge shows if an atom has its 'fair share' of electrons compared to neutral state.

image.alt: Formal charge calculation for CO₂ showing all atoms with zero formal charge
image.caption: Formal charge calculation: helps identify most stable Lewis structure.

principles.principle: Formal Charge Guidelines for Best Structure
principles.statement: Best Lewis structure has: (1) formal charges closest to zero, (2) negative formal charges on more electronegative atoms, (3) minimal charge separation.
principles.application: Selects most stable structure when multiple arrangements possible; predicts actual charge distribution.
principles.example: OCN⁻: [O-C≡N]⁻ (O=-1, C=0, N=0) is better than [O=C=N]⁻ (O=0, C=0, N=-1) because O is more electronegative than N.
principles.analogy: Like seating arrangement: put the 'negative' person (negative charge) in the seat that fits best (more electronegative atom).

image.alt: Comparison of Lewis structures with different formal charge distributions
image.caption: Formal charge guidelines: minimize charges, put negative on electronegative atoms.

principles.statement: Formal charge is a bookkeeping tool, not actual charge; actual charge distribution depends on electronegativity differences.
principles.application: Formal charge helps with Lewis structures; actual partial charges (δ⁺, δ⁻) depend on electronegativity.
principles.example: CO: C≡O with C=-1, O=+1 (formal), but actual δ⁺ on C, δ⁻ on O due to O's higher electronegativity.
principles.analogy: Like a map vs. terrain: formal charge is the map (simplified); actual charge is the terrain (reality).

image.alt: Comparison of formal charge vs. actual partial charge in CO
image.caption: Formal vs. actual charge: formal is bookkeeping; actual depends on electronegativity.

components.definition: Formal charge formula and interpretation for Lewis structure selection
components.interpretation: Formal charge = 0 is ideal; negative formal charge on more electronegative atoms is preferred; minimize formal charge magnitudes.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Formal Charges for CO₂
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the formal charge on each atom in CO₂ (O=C=O). Verify that the sum equals the molecular charge (0).

step_by_step.explanation: Identify valence electrons for each atom.
step_by_step.calculation: C: Group 14 → 4 valence e⁻; O: Group 16 → 6 valence e⁻ each

step_by_step.explanation: Count lone pair and bonding electrons for each atom.
step_by_step.calculation: C: 0 lone pair e⁻, 8 bonding e⁻ (4 bonds); O: 4 lone pair e⁻ each, 4 bonding e⁻ each (2 bonds)

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate formal charge for C.
Calculate formal charge for each O.
step_by_step.calculation: O: FC = 6 - (4 + ½×4) = 6 - 6 = 0; Sum: 0 + 0 + 0 = 0 ✓ (matches molecular charge)

worked_examples.final_answer: CO₂ formal charges: C = 0, each O = 0. Sum = 0 (matches neutral molecule). All formal charges = 0 confirms O=C=O is the most stable Lewis structure. This is an ideal structure with no formal charges.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Formal charge calculation to verify Lewis structure correctness

worked_examples.title: Using Formal Charge to Select Best Lewis Structure
worked_examples.problem: Two Lewis structures can be drawn for the thiocyanate ion (SCN⁻): [S-C≡N]⁻ and [S=C=N]⁻. Calculate formal charges for each and determine which is more stable.

step_by_step.explanation: Draw first structure [S-C≡N]⁻ and count electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 valence, 6 lone pair e⁻, 2 bonding e⁻; C: 4 valence, 0 lone, 8 bonding; N: 5 valence, 2 lone, 6 bonding

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate formal charges for [S-C≡N]⁻.
Draw second structure [S=C=N]⁻ and count electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 valence, 4 lone pair e⁻, 4 bonding e⁻; C: 4 valence, 0 lone, 8 bonding; N: 5 valence, 4 lone, 4 bonding

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate formal charges for [S=C=N]⁻ and compare.
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6-(4+2)=0; C: 4-(0+4)=0; N: 5-(4+2)=-1; Sum=-1 ✓; Structure 1 better: negative charge on N (EN=3.04) vs. S (EN=2.58)

worked_examples.final_answer: Structure 1 [S-C≡N]⁻: S=-1, C=0, N=0. Structure 2 [S=C=N]⁻: S=0, C=0, N=-1. Structure 1 is more stable because negative formal charge is on S (EN=2.58) which is less electronegative than N (EN=3.04). Actually, Structure 2 is preferred because N is more electronegative and better stabilizes negative charge. Both have same charge magnitudes, but Structure 2 places negative on more electronegative N.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using formal charge to select most stable Lewis structure from multiple possibilities

sections.introduction: Some molecules cannot be accurately represented by a single Lewis structure. Resonance structures are multiple valid Lewis structures that differ only in electron placement (not atom positions); the actual molecule is a hybrid of all resonance forms.

key_concept.definition: Resonance structures are multiple valid Lewis structures for the same molecule that differ only in electron placement (bond and lone pair positions), not atom positions. The actual molecule is a resonance hybrid of all structures.
key_concept.context: Resonance explains why some molecules have equal bond lengths despite Lewis structures showing different bond types; delocalized electrons provide extra stability.

principles.statement: Resonance structures must have: (1) same atom positions, (2) same total electrons, (3) valid Lewis structures, (4) only electrons move (not atoms).
principles.application: Identifies valid resonance structures; distinguishes resonance from isomers (which have different atom positions).
principles.example: O₃ (ozone): two resonance structures with double bond in different positions. Actual molecule has two identical O-O bonds intermediate between single and double.
principles.analogy: Like a mule: not a horse or donkey, but a hybrid of both; resonance hybrid is not one structure or another, but a blend.

image.alt: Diagram showing valid vs. invalid resonance structures
image.caption: Resonance rules: only electrons move, atoms stay fixed, all structures must be valid Lewis structures.

principles.statement: The actual molecule is a resonance hybrid of all valid structures, not switching between them; electrons are delocalized over multiple atoms.
principles.application: Explains equal bond lengths, extra stability (resonance energy), and charge distribution in molecules.
principles.example: Benzene (C₆H₆): two resonance structures with alternating double bonds. Actual molecule has 6 identical C-C bonds, all intermediate between single and double.
principles.analogy: Like a spinning fan: blades appear as a disk (hybrid), not individual blades (structures); molecule exists as hybrid, not switching between forms.

image.alt: Resonance structures and hybrid for ozone showing delocalized electrons
image.caption: Resonance hybrid: actual molecule is blend of all resonance structures, not switching between them.

principles.statement: More resonance structures = more stable molecule; resonance energy is the extra stability from electron delocalization.
principles.application: Predicts relative stability of molecules; explains why some compounds are unusually stable.
principles.example: Benzene: 2 resonance structures, resonance energy = 152 kJ/mol (extra stable). Carbonate (CO₃²⁻): 3 resonance structures, all C-O bonds equal length.
principles.analogy: Like sharing weight: more people carrying a load (more resonance structures) = more stable (less burden on each).

image.alt: Comparison of molecules with different numbers of resonance structures
image.caption: Resonance stability: more resonance structures = more stable molecule due to electron delocalization.

classifications.value: Two resonance structures with double bond in different positions
classifications.behavior: Double bond delocalized over both O-O positions; actual bonds are identical, intermediate between single and double.

examples.explanation: Structure 1: O=O-O with charges; Structure 2: O-O=O with charges reversed. Actual: both O-O bonds = 128 pm (between single 148 pm and double 121 pm).

image.alt: Two resonance structures for ozone with hybrid
image.caption: Ozone resonance: two structures, actual molecule has equal bond lengths.

classifications.value: Three resonance structures with double bond rotating among three O atoms
classifications.behavior: Double bond delocalized over all three C-O positions; all bonds identical length (129 pm).

examples.explanation: 3 structures with C=O in different positions. All C-O bonds = 129 pm (between single 143 pm and double 122 pm). Each O has -2/3 charge.

image.alt: Three resonance structures for carbonate ion
image.caption: Carbonate resonance: three structures, all C-O bonds equal length.

classifications.value: Two resonance structures with alternating double bonds in six-membered ring
classifications.behavior: π electrons delocalized over entire ring; all C-C bonds = 140 pm (between single 154 pm and double 134 pm).

examples.explanation: 2 structures with alternating C=C. Actual: all 6 C-C bonds identical (140 pm). Resonance energy = 152 kJ/mol (extra stable).

image.alt: Two resonance structures for benzene with delocalized ring
image.caption: Benzene resonance: two structures, all C-C bonds equal, exceptional stability.

classifications.value: Three resonance structures with double bond rotating among three O atoms
classifications.behavior: Double bond delocalized over all three N-O positions; all bonds identical length (124 pm).

examples.explanation: 3 structures with N=O in different positions. All N-O bonds = 124 pm. N has +1, each O has -2/3 charge (total -1).

image.alt: Three resonance structures for nitrate ion
image.caption: Nitrate resonance: three structures, all N-O bonds equal length.

worked_examples.title: Drawing Resonance Structures for Ozone (O₃)
worked_examples.problem: Draw all valid resonance structures for ozone (O₃). Calculate formal charges and explain why the actual molecule has equal bond lengths.

step_by_step.explanation: Count total valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: 3 O atoms × 6 e⁻ = 18 valence electrons

step_by_step.explanation: Draw first Lewis structure with one double bond.
step_by_step.calculation: O=O-O with central O having 1 lone pair, double-bonded O having 2 lone pairs, single-bonded O having 3 lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate formal charges for first structure.
step_by_step.calculation: Double-bonded O: 6-(4+2)=0; Central O: 6-(2+3)=+1; Single-bonded O: 6-(6+1)=-1

step_by_step.explanation: Draw second resonance structure by moving double bond.
step_by_step.calculation: O-O=O with charges reversed: single-bonded O=-1, central O=+1, double-bonded O=0

step_by_step.explanation: Explain resonance hybrid.
step_by_step.calculation: Actual molecule: both O-O bonds = 128 pm (between single 148 pm and double 121 pm); charge delocalized over terminal O atoms

worked_examples.final_answer: O₃ has 2 resonance structures: O=O-O ↔ O-O=O. Formal charges: double-bonded O=0, central O=+1, single-bonded O=-1 (reversed in second structure). Actual molecule is resonance hybrid with both O-O bonds equal (128 pm), intermediate between single and double. Negative charge delocalized over both terminal O atoms. This demonstrates resonance: actual structure is hybrid, not switching between forms.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Resonance structure drawing and hybrid concept for molecules with delocalized electrons

worked_examples.title: Drawing Resonance Structures for Carbonate Ion (CO₃²⁻)
worked_examples.problem: Draw all resonance structures for the carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻). Calculate formal charges and explain the equal C-O bond lengths.

step_by_step.explanation: Count total valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: C: 4 e⁻; 3 O: 18 e⁻; Charge: +2 e⁻; Total = 24 valence electrons

step_by_step.explanation: Draw first Lewis structure with one C=O double bond.
step_by_step.calculation: C central, 1 C=O, 2 C-O single bonds; complete octets with lone pairs

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate formal charges for first structure.
step_by_step.calculation: C: 4-(0+4)=0; Double-bonded O: 6-(4+2)=0; Single-bonded O: 6-(6+1)=-1 each

step_by_step.explanation: Draw remaining resonance structures by moving double bond.
step_by_step.calculation: 3 total structures with C=O in each of 3 positions; all equivalent

step_by_step.explanation: Explain resonance hybrid and bond lengths.
step_by_step.calculation: Actual: all 3 C-O bonds = 129 pm (between single 143 pm and double 122 pm); each O has -2/3 charge

worked_examples.final_answer: CO₃²⁻ has 3 resonance structures with C=O in each position. Formal charges: C=0, double-bonded O=0, single-bonded O=-1 (varies by structure). Actual molecule is resonance hybrid with all 3 C-O bonds equal (129 pm). Each O shares -2/3 charge (total -2). Three resonance structures provide extra stability through electron delocalization.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Resonance in polyatomic ions with multiple equivalent structures

sections.section_title: Exceptions to the Octet Rule in Covalent Lewis Structures
sections.introduction: While the octet rule applies to most main-group elements, important exceptions exist in covalent compounds. Some atoms have incomplete octets (fewer than 8 electrons), while others (Period 3 and below) can have expanded octets (more than 8 electrons).

key_concept.definition: Octet rule exceptions in covalent compounds include: incomplete octets (Be, B with <8 e⁻), expanded octets (Period 3+ elements with >8 e⁻ using d orbitals), and odd-electron molecules (free radicals with unpaired electrons).
key_concept.context: These exceptions occur due to atomic size, available orbitals, and electron count; understanding them is essential for accurate Lewis structure drawing.

classifications.value: Atoms with fewer than 8 valence electrons in stable molecules
characteristics: Common for Be (4 e⁻), B (6 e⁻)
classifications.behavior: Be and B don't have enough valence electrons to complete octet without forming charged species; accept electron pairs from other molecules.

examples.explanation: Be has 2 valence e⁻, forms 2 bonds with H. Be has only 4 e⁻ around it (incomplete octet). Linear molecule. Be is electron-deficient, acts as Lewis acid.

image.alt: BeH₂ Lewis structure showing incomplete octet on Be
image.caption: Incomplete octet: Be has only 4 valence electrons in BeH₂.

examples.explanation: B has 3 valence e⁻, forms 3 bonds with F. B has only 6 e⁻ around it (incomplete octet). Trigonal planar. Reacts with NH₃ to complete octet: BF₃ + NH₃ → F₃B-NH₃.

image.alt: BF₃ Lewis structure showing incomplete octet on B
image.caption: Incomplete octet: B has only 6 valence electrons in BF₃, accepts electron pair from NH₃.

classifications.value: Atoms with more than 8 valence electrons (Period 3 and below)
characteristics: Elements with available d orbitals (P, S, Cl, etc.)
classifications.behavior: Period 3+ elements can use empty d orbitals to accommodate more than 8 electrons; enables compounds like SF₆, PCl₅, ClF₃.

examples.explanation: S has 6 valence e⁻, forms 6 bonds with F. S has 12 e⁻ around it (expanded octet). Octahedral geometry. S uses 3d orbitals to accommodate extra electrons.

image.alt: SF₆ Lewis structure showing expanded octet on S
image.caption: Expanded octet: S has 12 valence electrons in SF₆ using d orbitals.

examples.explanation: P has 5 valence e⁻, forms 5 bonds with Cl. P has 10 e⁻ around it (expanded octet). Trigonal bipyramidal geometry. Used in organic synthesis.

image.alt: PCl₅ Lewis structure showing expanded octet on P
image.caption: Expanded octet: P has 10 valence electrons in PCl₅.

examples.explanation: Cl has 7 valence e⁻, forms 3 bonds with F, has 2 lone pairs. Cl has 10 e⁻ around it (expanded octet). T-shaped geometry. Extremely reactive oxidizing agent.

image.alt: ClF₃ Lewis structure showing expanded octet on Cl
image.caption: Expanded octet: Cl has 10 valence electrons in ClF₃ with 2 lone pairs.

classifications.value: Molecules with unpaired electrons, cannot satisfy octet for all atoms
classifications.behavior: Cannot pair all electrons; one atom has 7 electrons instead of 8; highly reactive due to unpaired electron seeking to pair.

examples.explanation: N has 5 e⁻, O has 6 e⁻, total = 11 e⁻ (odd). N has 7 e⁻ around it (incomplete). Paramagnetic (unpaired e⁻). Important signaling molecule in biology.

image.alt: NO Lewis structure showing unpaired electron
image.caption: Odd-electron molecule: NO has 11 valence electrons, one unpaired.

examples.explanation: N has 5 e⁻, 2 O have 12 e⁻, total = 17 e⁻ (odd). N has 7 e⁻ around it. Brown gas, air pollutant, paramagnetic. Dimerizes to N₂O₄ to pair electrons.

image.alt: NO₂ Lewis structure showing unpaired electron on N
image.caption: Odd-electron molecule: NO₂ has 17 valence electrons, highly reactive.

principles.statement: Period 2 elements (C, N, O, F) cannot have expanded octets because they lack accessible d orbitals; maximum is 8 electrons in 2s and 2p orbitals.
principles.application: Explains why CF₄ exists but CF₆ doesn't; why OF₂ exists but OF₄ doesn't; limits bonding patterns for Period 2 elements.
principles.example: C max = 4 bonds (8 e⁻); N max = 4 bonds (8 e⁻ in NH₄⁺); O max = 2 bonds + 2 lone pairs (8 e⁻); F max = 1 bond + 3 lone pairs (8 e⁻).
principles.analogy: Like parking spaces: Period 2 has only 4 spaces (1 s + 3 p orbitals); Period 3+ has 9 spaces (1 s + 3 p + 5 d orbitals).

image.alt: Orbital diagram showing Period 2 vs. Period 3 available orbitals
image.caption: Period 2 limitation: no d orbitals available, maximum 8 electrons in valence shell.

principles.statement: Expanded octets are most stable when central atom is large (Period 3+) and bonded to small, electronegative atoms (F, O, Cl).
principles.application: Predicts which elements form expanded octet compounds: SF₆ stable, OF₆ impossible; PCl₅ stable, NCl₅ impossible.
principles.example: S (Period 3) forms SF₆ (12 e⁻); O (Period 2) cannot form OF₆. P (Period 3) forms PCl₅ (10 e⁻); N (Period 2) cannot form NCl₅.
principles.analogy: Like a host at a party: larger host (bigger atom) can accommodate more guests (electrons); small host (Period 2) has limited capacity.

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure with Expanded Octet: SF₄
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for SF₄ (sulfur tetrafluoride). Show the expanded octet on sulfur and calculate the formal charge on each atom.

step_by_step.explanation: Count total valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 e⁻; F: 4 atoms × 7 e⁻ = 28 e⁻; Total = 34 valence electrons

step_by_step.explanation: Arrange atoms with S as center.
step_by_step.calculation: S center with 4 F atoms around it

step_by_step.explanation: Draw single bonds between S and each F.
step_by_step.calculation: 4 bonds × 2 e⁻ = 8 e⁻ used; 34 - 8 = 26 e⁻ remaining

step_by_step.explanation: Complete octets on F atoms with lone pairs.
step_by_step.calculation: 4 F × 6 e⁻ (3 lone pairs each) = 24 e⁻; 26 - 24 = 2 e⁻ remaining

step_by_step.explanation: Place remaining electrons on S as lone pair.
step_by_step.calculation: 2 e⁻ = 1 lone pair on S; S has 10 e⁻ total (4 bonds + 1 lone pair) = expanded octet

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate formal charges.
step_by_step.calculation: S: 6 - (2 + ½×8) = 0; F: 7 - (6 + ½×2) = 0. All formal charges = 0 ✓

worked_examples.final_answer: SF₄ Lewis structure: S center with 4 F atoms single-bonded, 1 lone pair on S. S has 10 valence electrons (expanded octet using 3d orbitals). Seesaw molecular geometry. All formal charges = 0, confirming stable structure. S is Period 3, so expanded octet is possible.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Expanded octet Lewis structure drawing with formal charge verification

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure with Incomplete Octet: BF₃
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for BF₃ (boron trifluoride). Explain why boron has an incomplete octet and how it can complete the octet.

step_by_step.explanation: Count total valence electrons.
step_by_step.calculation: B: 3 e⁻; F: 3 atoms × 7 e⁻ = 21 e⁻; Total = 24 valence electrons

step_by_step.explanation: Arrange atoms with B as center.
step_by_step.calculation: B center with 3 F atoms around it

step_by_step.explanation: Draw single bonds between B and each F.
step_by_step.calculation: 3 bonds × 2 e⁻ = 6 e⁻ used; 24 - 6 = 18 e⁻ remaining

step_by_step.explanation: Complete octets on F atoms with lone pairs.
step_by_step.calculation: 3 F × 6 e⁻ (3 lone pairs each) = 18 e⁻; 18 - 18 = 0 e⁻ remaining; B has only 6 e⁻ (incomplete octet)

step_by_step.explanation: Explain incomplete octet and how to complete it.
step_by_step.calculation: B has 6 e⁻ (incomplete); can accept lone pair from Lewis base like NH₃: BF₃ + NH₃ → F₃B-NH₃ (B achieves octet)

worked_examples.final_answer: BF₃ Lewis structure: B center with 3 F atoms single-bonded, no lone pairs on B. B has only 6 valence electrons (incomplete octet). This is stable because B is electron-deficient and acts as Lewis acid. B can complete octet by accepting electron pair from Lewis base like NH₃, forming coordinate covalent bond.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Incomplete octet Lewis structure with Lewis acid-base completion

sections.section_title: Common Errors in Drawing Covalent Lewis Structures
sections.introduction: Students often make specific errors when drawing Lewis structures for covalent compounds. Recognizing and avoiding these common mistakes ensures accurate representations of covalent bonding.

key_concept.definition: Common errors in covalent Lewis structures include: wrong electron counts, incomplete octets without justification, incorrect bond types, missing lone pairs, and confusing resonance with isomers.
key_concept.context: Understanding these common errors and their corrections helps develop accurate Lewis structure drawing skills and deeper understanding of covalent bonding.

classifications.value: Incorrect total valence electron count leading to wrong structures
classifications.behavior: Results in too many or too few electrons; octets can't be satisfied; structure is invalid.

examples.process: Error: CO₂ with 14 e⁻ instead of 16 e⁻
examples.explanation: Incorrect: counted C(4) + O(6) = 10 e⁻ (forgot second O). Correct: C(4) + 2×O(6) = 16 e⁻. Always count all atoms!

image.alt: Comparison of correct and incorrect electron counts
image.caption: Electron count error: must count all atoms in molecule.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: N(5) + 4×H(1) = 9 e⁻. Correct: N(5) + 4×H(1) - 1(charge) = 8 e⁻. Subtract electrons for positive charges!

image.alt: Correct electron counting for ions
image.caption: Charge error: subtract electrons for positive charges, add for negative.

classifications.value: Central atom lacks octet when it shouldn't (not Be or B)
classifications.behavior: Structure is unstable; should form double/triple bonds to complete octets.

examples.process: Error: CO₂ with single bonds only
examples.explanation: Incorrect: O-C-O with C having only 4 e⁻. Correct: O=C=O with C having 8 e⁻. Form double bonds when central atom lacks octet!

image.alt: Correct and incorrect CO₂ structures
image.caption: Octet error: must form multiple bonds to complete central atom octet.

examples.explanation: Incorrect: N-N with each N having 6 e⁻. Correct: N≡N with each N having 8 e⁻. Form triple bond!

image.alt: Correct N≡N vs. incorrect N-N structure
image.caption: Bond type error: N₂ requires triple bond for octets.

classifications.value: Forgot to draw lone pairs on atoms with non-bonding electrons
characteristics: O, N, F without lone pairs
Octet appears complete but electrons missing
classifications.behavior: Misrepresents electron distribution; affects molecular geometry prediction; formal charges wrong.

examples.process: Error: H₂O without lone pairs on O
examples.explanation: Incorrect: H-O-H with no dots. Correct: H-O-H with 2 lone pairs on O. O needs 8 e⁻ total (2 bonds + 2 lone pairs).

image.alt: Correct H₂O with lone pairs vs. incorrect without
image.caption: Lone pair error: must show all non-bonding electrons as dots.

examples.process: Error: NH₃ without lone pair on N
examples.explanation: Incorrect: N with 3 H bonds, no lone pair. Correct: N with 3 H bonds + 1 lone pair. N needs 8 e⁻ total.

image.alt: Correct NH₃ with lone pair vs. incorrect without
image.caption: Lone pair error: N in NH₃ has 1 lone pair affecting geometry.

classifications.value: Drawing structures with different atom positions as resonance
classifications.behavior: Resonance only involves electron movement; atom positions must stay fixed; different atom positions = different compounds (isomers).

examples.process: Error: HCN and HNC as resonance
examples.explanation: Incorrect: H-C≡N ↔ H-N≡C called resonance. Correct: These are isomers (different compounds), not resonance. Atoms moved!

image.alt: Resonance vs. isomers comparison
image.caption: Resonance error: atoms must stay fixed; only electrons move in resonance.

examples.process: Error: Ethanol and dimethyl ether as resonance
examples.explanation: Incorrect: CH₃CH₂OH ↔ CH₃OCH₃ called resonance. Correct: These are structural isomers (different connectivity), not resonance forms.

image.alt: Ethanol vs. dimethyl ether showing different connectivity
image.caption: Isomer error: different atom connectivity = isomers, not resonance.

principles.principle: Verification Checklist for Covalent Lewis Structures
principles.statement: Always verify covalent Lewis structures by checking: correct electron count, all atoms have octets (except exceptions), formal charges minimized, lone pairs shown.
principles.application: Systematic verification prevents common errors; ensures accurate representation; builds good drawing habits.
principles.example: CO₂ check: 16 e⁻ ✓, C has 8 e⁻ (4 bonds) ✓, each O has 8 e⁻ (2 bonds + 2 lone pairs) ✓, all formal charges = 0 ✓, lone pairs shown ✓.
principles.analogy: Like a pre-flight checklist: verify each item before considering the structure complete and correct.

worked_examples.title: Identifying and Correcting Lewis Structure Errors
worked_examples.problem: Identify all errors in this Lewis structure for CO₃²⁻: C center with 3 single bonds to O, no double bonds, no formal charges shown. Draw the correct structure.

step_by_step.explanation: Identify all errors in the given structure.
step_by_step.calculation: Error 1: Only single bonds (C has 6 e⁻, incomplete octet). Error 2: No formal charges shown. Error 3: No resonance indicated.

step_by_step.explanation: Correct the electron count.
step_by_step.calculation: C: 4 e⁻; 3 O: 18 e⁻; Charge: +2 e⁻; Total = 24 valence electrons ✓

step_by_step.explanation: Form double bond to complete C octet.
step_by_step.calculation: Move 1 lone pair from one O to form C=O double bond; C now has 8 e⁻

step_by_step.explanation: Add formal charges and show resonance.
step_by_step.calculation: C: 0, double-bonded O: 0, single-bonded O: -1 each; Draw all 3 resonance structures; Add brackets and -2 charge

worked_examples.final_answer: Errors identified: (1) C has incomplete octet (only 6 e⁻), (2) no double bonds formed, (3) no formal charges shown, (4) no resonance indicated. Correct structure: 3 resonance structures with C=O in each position, formal charges C=0, double-bonded O=0, single-bonded O=-1 each, overall -2 charge in brackets. All C-O bonds equal in actual molecule (resonance hybrid).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Error identification and correction in covalent Lewis structures with systematic verification

summary: Lewis structures for covalent compounds show electron sharing through a systematic 5-step procedure: (1) Count total valence electrons (add for negative charge, subtract for positive), (2) Arrange atoms with least electronegative as center (H always terminal), (3) Draw single bonds (2 e⁻ per bond), (4) Distribute remaining electrons as lone pairs (outer atoms first), (5) Form multiple bonds if central atom lacks octet. Bond types: single (1 line, 2 e⁻, longest, weakest, free rotation), double (2 lines, 4 e⁻, intermediate), triple (3 lines, 6 e⁻, shortest, strongest, rigid). Formal charge = valence e⁻ - (lone pair e⁻ + ½ bonding e⁻); best structure has formal charges closest to zero with negative charges on more electronegative atoms. Resonance structures are multiple valid Lewis structures differing only in electron placement (not atom positions); actual molecule is resonance hybrid with delocalized electrons and equal bond lengths (O₃: 2 structures, CO₃²⁻: 3 structures, C₆H₆: 2 structures). Octet rule exceptions: incomplete octets (Be: 4 e⁻, B: 6 e⁻), expanded octets (Period 3+ elements like S, P, Cl can exceed 8 e⁻ using d orbitals), odd-electron molecules (free radicals like NO, NO₂ with unpaired electrons). Period 2 elements cannot expand octet (no d orbitals). Common errors: wrong electron counts, incomplete octets without forming multiple bonds, missing lone pairs, confusing resonance with isomers. Verification checklist: correct electron count ✓, all octets satisfied (or justified exception) ✓, formal charges minimized ✓, lone pairs shown ✓, resonance indicated when applicable ✓. Understanding covalent Lewis structures enables prediction of molecular geometry, bond properties, reactivity, and stability for the vast majority of chemical compounds.
